Exit Strategy Benchmark

§ 01Owner's Goals Assessment

Before evaluating exit strategies, it is essential to understand what matters most to the owner beyond financial return. The following assessment captures the non-financial priorities that shape the right path forward.

Legacy Preservation
{{legacyScore}} / 10
Employee Welfare Priority
{{employeeWelfareScore}} / 10
Community Impact
{{communityScore}} / 10
Control Preference
{{controlPreference}}
Timeline Preference
{{timelinePreference}}
Financial Priority
{{financialPriority}}
Additional goals: {{ownerAdditionalGoals}}
§ 02Exit Strategy Comparison

The table below compares the five most common exit strategies across the dimensions that matter to business owners. Each strategy is evaluated on its typical outcomes — actual results vary by deal structure, company performance, and market conditions.

Dimension ESOP PE Recap Strategic Sale Mgmt Buyout Family Succession
Seller Proceeds Fair market value; may include seller note Partial liquidity; upside on second bite Highest headline price Below market; seller-financed Below market; gift/estate structure
Tax Treatment §1042 deferral eligible; C-corp deductions Capital gains on sale portion Capital gains; asset vs. stock sale Capital gains on installment sale Estate/gift tax planning
Timeline 90–150 days typical 60–120 days 6–12 months 3–6 months Years (gradual transition)
Employee Impact Positive — ownership stake Variable — restructuring risk High uncertainty Neutral to positive Positive — continuity
Control Retention High — board, management retained Shared with PE partner None — full exit Partial — seller may stay on Full — family retains
Legacy Preservation High — name, culture, community Moderate — PE has its playbook Low — acquirer integrates Moderate — depends on team Highest — family continuity
Certainty of Close High — no buyer search needed Moderate — diligence heavy Variable — buyer dependent Low — financing risk High — if family willing
§ 03ESOP-Specific Advantages

When an owner's goals align with legacy, employees, and community, the ESOP structure offers advantages that no other exit strategy can match:

A.§1042 capital gains deferral. Sellers who reinvest proceeds into Qualified Replacement Property within 12 months can defer capital gains indefinitely. No other exit strategy offers this tax treatment.
B.Employee retention and motivation. ESOP participants accumulate retirement wealth tied to company performance. Studies show employee-owned companies have 25% higher productivity and significantly lower turnover.
C.C-corp tax benefits. A 100% ESOP-owned C-corp pays no federal income tax on the portion attributable to ESOP ownership. The company can deduct ESOP contributions used to repay acquisition debt.
D.Gradual transition option. The owner can sell a partial stake initially and transition fully over time, maintaining operational control and income during the process.
E.No outside buyer risk. The ESOP trust is the buyer. There is no risk of deal collapse due to buyer financing failure, renegotiation, or cultural mismatch.
F.Community and jobs preservation. The company remains independent, locally managed, and committed to its existing workforce and community. No plant closures, no headquarters relocations.
§ 04Strategic Fit Summary
Assessment: Based on the owner's stated priorities — legacy preservation ({{legacyScore}}/10), employee welfare ({{employeeWelfareScore}}/10), and community impact ({{communityScore}}/10) — the ESOP structure offers the strongest alignment with non-financial goals while still delivering fair market value. {{fitSummaryNarrative}}
§ 05Recommended Next Steps
1.Pre-flight checklist. Complete the Forhemit pre-flight assessment to determine ESOP feasibility, including EBITDA threshold, employee count, and corporate structure requirements.
2.Tax consultation. Share the §1042 explainer with the seller's CPA to evaluate the specific tax benefits applicable to this transaction.
3.Net proceeds model. Run the interactive net proceeds calculator to compare after-tax outcomes across exit strategies using the company's actual financials.
Next step: If the owner is interested in exploring the ESOP path, the next document is the Pre-flight Cover Letter and Checklist, which initiates the formal qualification process.
—Forhemit Transition Stewardship
